title: The Yogyakarta Principles are adopted
description: A group of international human-rights experts meeting in Yogyakarta, Indonesia, adopts a set of principles on applying international human-rights law to sexual orientation and gender identity. The document becomes a global reference point for LGBTQ rights advocacy.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Isle of Man repeals its Section 28 equivalent
description: The Isle of Man removes its local counterpart of Section 28, the provision that had barred public authorities from portraying homosexuality positively.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jewish movements ordain openly lesbian and transgender clergy
description: Chaya Gusfield and Lori Klein become the first openly lesbian rabbis ordained within the Jewish Renewal movement, Conservative Judaism votes to accept openly lesbian rabbis and cantors, and Elliot Kukla becomes the first openly transgender person ordained by Hebrew Union College–Jewish Institute of Religion.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Patricia Todd becomes Alabama's first openly gay elected official
description: Voters send Patricia Todd of Birmingham to the Alabama House of Representatives, making her the state's first openly gay public officeholder.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Kim Coco Iwamoto wins statewide office in Hawaii
description: Kim Coco Iwamoto is elected to the Hawaii Board of Education, becoming at the time the highest-ranking openly transgender elected official in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Bernard Lynch enters a civil partnership as a Catholic priest
description: Irish priest Bernard Lynch becomes the first Catholic priest known to enter a civil partnership. His religious order expelled him in 2011, and he legally married his husband in 2016.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Aruba required to recognize Dutch same-sex marriages
description: Following a ruling of the Dutch Supreme Court, same-sex marriages performed in the Netherlands must be registered in Aruba, although the island does not perform such marriages itself.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Curaçao recognizes same-sex marriages from the Netherlands
description: Same-sex marriages concluded in the Netherlands gain recognition in Curaçao, then part of the Netherlands Antilles, though such marriages cannot be performed locally.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sint Maarten recognizes same-sex marriages from the Netherlands
description: Same-sex marriages performed in the Netherlands gain recognition in Sint Maarten, then part of the Netherlands Antilles, though the island does not conduct such marriages itself.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Coahuila enacts civil unions for same-sex couples
description: The Mexican state of Coahuila passes its civil-pact law and puts it into effect, becoming the first Mexican state to give same-sex couples legal recognition.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mexico City's civil-union law takes effect
description: The Ley de Sociedad de Convivencia, approved in late 2006, comes into force in Mexico City, allowing same-sex couples to register civil unions.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Switzerland's registered partnership law takes effect
description: The federal registered partnership law approved by Swiss voters in 2005 comes into force, giving same-sex couples nationwide legal recognition without adoption rights.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: New Jersey civil unions take effect
description: New Jersey's civil-union law comes into force, extending the state-level rights and responsibilities of marriage to same-sex couples.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Hungary passes a registered partnership law
description: The Hungarian parliament approves legislation creating registered partnerships for same-sex couples; after constitutional review, a revised version came into force in 2009.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: New Hampshire approves civil unions
description: New Hampshire lawmakers pass a civil-union bill granting same-sex couples state-level rights equivalent to marriage, effective the following year.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Uruguay passes a national civil-union law
description: Uruguay's parliament approves the Ley de Unión Concubinaria, the first national law in Latin America recognizing same-sex couples; joint adoption was initially excluded.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Washington State enacts domestic partnerships
description: Washington State passes and brings into effect a domestic-partnership registry offering same-sex couples a limited set of legal protections.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Colombia grants partnership rights to same-sex couples
description: Colombia extends limited partnership rights to same-sex couples, following constitutional court decisions granting property and social-security protections to de facto unions.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: South Australia's domestic partnership scheme takes effect
description: Legislation recognizing domestic partners comes into force in South Australia, giving same-sex couples limited legal recognition.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: United Kingdom outlaws sexual-orientation discrimination in services
description: The Equality Act (Sexual Orientation) Regulations come into force across the United Kingdom, banning discrimination on grounds of sexual orientation in the provision of goods, facilities and services.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Seven US states expand anti-discrimination protections
description: Colorado, Iowa, Kansas, Michigan, Ohio, Oregon and Vermont adopt measures barring discrimination based on sexual orientation and/or gender identity, in scopes ranging from public-sector employment to the private sector.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Portugal equalizes the age of consent
description: A revision of Portugal's penal code removes the higher age of consent that had applied to same-sex sexual relations, setting a single age for all.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: South Africa equalizes the age of consent
description: South Africa's Criminal Law (Sexual Offences and Related Matters) Amendment Act sets a uniform age of consent of 16, ending the higher threshold previously applied to same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jersey equalizes the age of consent
description: The UK Crown dependency of Jersey lowers the age of consent for sex between men to match the general age of consent.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Vanuatu equalizes the age of consent
description: Vanuatu removes the age-of-consent distinction between same-sex and different-sex relations, applying a single standard to all.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Nepal's Supreme Court orders an end to laws criminalizing homosexuality
description: Nepal's Supreme Court directs the government to end discrimination against sexual and gender minorities and to repeal laws penalizing same-sex conduct, effectively decriminalizing homosexuality.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Tokelau decriminalizes sex between men
description: The New Zealand territory of Tokelau removes criminal penalties for consensual sexual relations between men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Istanbul pride march recorded as a first for a Muslim-majority country
description: A gay pride parade takes place in Istanbul; the timeline records it as the first such parade held in a Muslim-majority country, although smaller marches had been held in the city in earlier years.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: US presidential candidates attend the first LGBT-focused forum
description: The cable channel Logo hosts the first US presidential forum devoted specifically to LGBT issues; six Democratic candidates take part, while Republican candidates decline the invitation.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Candis Cayne plays a recurring transgender role in prime time
description: On the ABC drama Dirty Sexy Money, Candis Cayne portrays Carmelita Rainer, becoming the first openly transgender actress to play a recurring transgender character on US prime-time television.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Symbolic same-sex wedding held in Hanoi
description: A Japanese man and an Irish man hold a wedding ceremony in Hanoi, described as Vietnam's first gay wedding between foreign nationals. The event, without legal effect, draws wide attention among gay and lesbian Vietnamese.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jalda Rebling ordained as Jewish Renewal's first openly lesbian cantor
description: German cantor Jalda Rebling, born in the Netherlands, becomes the first openly lesbian cantor ordained by the Jewish Renewal movement.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Toba Spitzer becomes first openly LGBT head of a rabbinical assembly
description: Rabbi Toba Spitzer is elected president of the Reconstructionist movement's rabbinical assembly at its convention in Scottsdale, Arizona — the first openly lesbian or gay person to lead such a body.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Joy Ladin becomes first openly transgender professor at an Orthodox school
description: Yeshiva University's Stern College for Women keeps poet and scholar Joy Ladin on its faculty through her gender transition, making her the first openly transgender professor at an Orthodox Jewish institution.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Amaranta Gómez Regalado breaks ground in Mexican politics
description: Muxe activist Amaranta Gómez Regalado, associated with the México Posible party, is recorded as the first transsexual person to appear in Mexico's Congress; her congressional candidacy dates back to 2003.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Ellen DeGeneres hosts the Academy Awards
description: Ellen DeGeneres presents the Academy Awards ceremony, the first openly lesbian performer to host the Oscars.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Los Angeles street renamed for Betty Berzon
description: Ventura Place in Studio City is renamed Dr. Betty Berzon Place in honor of the psychotherapist and author, the first California street officially dedicated to a known lesbian.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Theresa Sparks elected to lead the San Francisco Police Commission
description: By a single vote, Theresa Sparks becomes president of the San Francisco Police Commission — the first openly transgender person elected to head a San Francisco commission and the city's highest-ranking openly transgender official.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: California briefly opens marriage to same-sex couples
description: After the state supreme court's In re Marriage Cases ruling, same-sex couples marry in California from mid-2008 until voters approve Proposition 8 in November of that year.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Connecticut legalizes same-sex marriage
description: Connecticut's supreme court rules that same-sex couples must be allowed to marry, and weddings begin in November 2008.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mashantucket Pequot Tribal Nation recognizes same-sex marriage
description: The timeline records the Mashantucket Pequot Tribal Nation in Connecticut as adopting same-sex marriage; other accounts date the tribe's marriage law to 2010.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Norway passes gender-neutral marriage law
description: Norway's parliament approves a gender-neutral marriage act granting same-sex couples full marriage and joint adoption rights; the law took effect in January 2009.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Australian Capital Territory introduces civil partnerships
description: The Australian Capital Territory passes and implements a civil-partnership scheme allowing same-sex couples to formalize their relationships.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Ecuador recognizes same-sex civil unions
description: Ecuador's new constitution, approved by referendum, recognizes civil unions for same-sex couples while excluding joint adoption.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Washington State expands domestic-partnership rights
description: Washington State broadens its domestic-partnership law, adding further rights for registered same-sex couples beyond the 2007 legislation.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: New Hampshire civil unions take effect
description: The civil-union law passed in 2007 comes into force in New Hampshire, letting same-sex couples enter unions carrying state-level marriage rights.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Uruguay's civil-union law takes effect
description: The Ley de Unión Concubinaria comes into force, making Uruguay the first Latin American country with a national framework recognizing same-sex couples; joint adoption followed later.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Victoria's relationships register takes effect
description: The Australian state of Victoria brings its relationships register into operation, giving same-sex couples formal legal recognition.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Oregon domestic partnerships take effect
description: Oregon's domestic-partnership law, passed in 2007, comes into force after a court-ordered delay, opening registration to same-sex couples.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Arizona and California voters approve same-sex marriage bans
description: Ballot measures in Arizona (Proposition 102) and California (Proposition 8) amend the state constitutions to restrict marriage to different-sex couples, ending the marriages that had begun in California earlier that year.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Florida bans same-sex marriage and civil unions
description: Florida voters approve Amendment 2, a constitutional amendment barring both same-sex marriage and any legal union treated as equivalent to marriage.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Uruguay legalizes adoption by same-sex couples
description: Uruguay allows couples in civil unions, including same-sex couples, to adopt jointly — the first Latin American country to do so. The timeline lists this under 2008, but the law passed in 2009.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Arkansas bars unmarried couples from adopting and fostering
description: Arkansas voters approve Act 1, blocking unmarried cohabiting couples — including same-sex couples, who could not marry — from adopting or fostering children. The state supreme court struck the measure down in 2011.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: California strengthens anti-discrimination protections
description: California adds further statutory protections against discrimination on the basis of sexual orientation and gender identity.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Nicaragua decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Nicaragua's new penal code comes into force without the former sodomy provision, ending the criminalization of consensual same-sex relations.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Panama decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Panama repeals the decree that had criminalized consensual same-sex sexual relations, removing its last such penal provision.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Bulgaria holds its first pride parade
description: Activists march in Sofia in the first gay pride parade held in Bulgaria.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Matthew Mitcham wins Olympic gold as an openly gay athlete
description: Australian diver Matthew Mitcham takes gold at the Beijing Games, the first openly gay man to win an Olympic gold medal.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Kosovo's constitution bans sexual-orientation discrimination
description: Newly independent Kosovo adopts a constitution that explicitly lists sexual orientation among protected grounds against discrimination, the first constitution in Eastern Europe to do so.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First same-sex civil marriages performed in Greece
description: The mayor of the island of Tilos marries two same-sex couples, one male and one female, using a gap in Greek marriage law; the supreme court prosecutor and the justice minister declare the unions void.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sam Adams elected mayor of Portland, Oregon
description: Portland voters elect Sam Adams, making him the first openly gay mayor of the city and of any top-30 US city by population.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Annise Parker elected mayor of Houston
description: Houston voters choose Annise Parker as mayor, the first openly lesbian or gay person to lead the city. The election took place in December 2009, although this timeline lists it under 2008.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Kate Brown becomes first openly bisexual statewide officeholder in the US
description: Oregon voters elect Kate Brown as secretary of state, making her the first openly bisexual person to hold statewide office in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Stu Rasmussen elected as America's first openly transgender mayor
description: Voters in Silverton, Oregon elect Stu Rasmussen as mayor, the first openly transgender person elected to a mayoralty in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Angie Zapata murdered in Colorado
description: Transgender woman Angie Zapata is killed in Greeley, Colorado. Her killer, Allen Andrade, is later convicted of first-degree murder and a bias-motivated crime — the first US hate-crime conviction for the killing of a transgender victim.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: US Congress holds first hearing on transgender workplace discrimination
description: The House Subcommittee on Health, Employment, Labor, and Pensions convenes the first congressional hearing on discrimination against transgender people in the workplace.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Rachel Maddow debuts as prime-time news anchor
description: MSNBC launches The Rachel Maddow Show, making Maddow the first openly gay or lesbian anchor of a major US prime-time news program.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Kay Ryan named United States Poet Laureate
description: The Library of Congress appoints Kay Ryan as Poet Laureate, the first openly lesbian person to hold the post.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sweden legalizes same-sex marriage
description: Sweden's parliament adopts a gender-neutral marriage law with joint adoption rights, and same-sex couples begin marrying in May 2009.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Iowa legalizes same-sex marriage
description: In Varnum v. Brien, the Iowa Supreme Court unanimously strikes down the state's marriage ban, and same-sex couples begin marrying in April 2009.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Vermont legalizes same-sex marriage by legislation
description: Vermont's legislature overrides the governor's veto to enact same-sex marriage, the first US state to adopt it by statute rather than court ruling.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 21st century (CC BY-SA 4.0)
